ground_truth.title: AI Coding Agents Tested: GPT-5 Excels in Minesweeper Challenge
ground_truth.author: Maya Perez
ground_truth.main_content: Ars Technica tested four AI coding agents—OpenAI's GPT-5 Codex, Google's Gemini Advanced, Anthropic's Claude 3.5 Sonnet, and Meta's Llama 3.1—on recreating Minesweeper in Python, revealing strengths like polished outputs and pitfalls such as bugs and inefficiencies. GPT-5 excelled, underscoring AI's potential and limitations in software innovation. OpenAI's GPT-5 Codex emerged as the standout, producing a fully playable game that included features like variable difficulty levels and sound effects. Google Gemini Advanced required multiple iterations to fix bugs in its flood-fill algorithm for revealing cells. Anthropic's Claude 3.5 Sonnet took a more conservative approach, delivering clean, well-commented code but lacking in user interface polish. Meta's Llama 3.1 struggled the most, generating code with syntax errors and failing to properly randomize mine locations. Beyond the individual results, the Minesweeper test raises questions about reliability in real-world applications as AI agents become more autonomous.
with: Ars Technica tested four AI coding agents—OpenAI's GPT-5 Codex, Google's Gemini Advanced, Anthropic's Claude 3.5 Sonnet, and Meta's Llama 3.1—on recreating Minesweeper in Python
OpenAI's GPT-5 Codex emerged as the standout, producing a fully playable game that included features like variable difficulty levels and sound effects
Google's Gemini Advanced, while competent, required multiple iterations to fix bugs in its flood-fill algorithm for revealing adjacent cells
Meta's Llama 3.1 struggled the most, generating code with syntax errors and failing to properly randomize mine locations
The experiment also ties into emerging trends like vibe coding, a term popularized in discussions on platforms such as Reddit's r/aiwars
